risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "To avoid biased allocation, randomisation is undertaken by central allocation using a computerized random number generator ... randomisation is performed to assign patients, in a 1:1 allocation ratio ... with stratification by sex, age (under or over 20 years old), and assumed duration of illness ... via the minimisation method." Comment: This describes a centralized computerized random allocation process with stratification/minimization, which is an adequate random sequence generation method and supports a low risk judgement.

Quote: "To avoid biased allocation, randomisation is undertaken by central allocation using a computerized random number generator at the Internet Data and Information Center for Medical Research (INDICE), provided by the University Hospital Medical Information Network (UMIN) in Japan." Comment: Central allocation is explicitly stated, which is an adequate concealment mechanism preventing recruiters from foreseeing the next assignment before enrollment.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Due to the nature of the study, neither the patient nor staff can be blinded." Comment: Participants and the personnel delivering the intervention were explicitly not blinded. Because the intervention is a shared decision-making program and the main outcomes include self-reported patient satisfaction, attitudes, and other patient-reported measures, knowledge of allocation could plausibly affect outcomes across the study overall, so this domain is at high risk of performance bias.

Quote: "Due to the nature of the study, neither the patient nor staff can be blinded." and "The primary outcome is the patient’s satisfaction at discharge as assessed by the Japanese version of the Client Satisfaction Questionnaire (CSQ-8J) ... a self-report scale"; also "The Japanese version of the Drug Attitude Inventory-10 (DAI-10) ... is a 10-item self-report instrument". Comment: The outcome assessor for the main patient-reported outcomes is the patient themselves (self-report), and participants were explicitly not blinded. Because satisfaction and medication attitudes are subjective and directly measurable by unblinded participants, knowledge of allocation could influence outcome measurement, so detection bias is high. Although symptom severity is assessed by the ward doctor, the study also includes unblinded patient-reported outcomes, and under the worst-outcome rule the domain is judged on the most vulnerable outcome.

Summary: This is a study protocol describing planned analyses rather than completed outcome data. It states that the trial "will be analysed on an intention-to-treat principle" and that "Multiple imputation methods will be used for imputing any missing information regarding outcome measures," but it does not report any actual withdrawals, exclusions, or per-arm loss to follow-up numbers. Comment: Because no observed attrition data are provided, the risk of bias from incomplete outcome data cannot be judged from the available text; the protocol's planned ITT/multiple imputation does not substitute for reported follow-up rates.
